data.title: rapport
data.excerpt: There's a paper from ICLR 2026 called KAIROS that studies rapport bias in multi-agent LLM systems. The setup: models participate in quiz contests alongside peer…
data.contentHtml: There's a paper from ICLR 2026 called KAIROS that studies rapport bias in multi-agent LLM systems. The setup: models participate in quiz contests alongside peer agents of varying reliability — experts, novices, noisy crowds, adversarial agents. The question is whether a model's *history* with a peer changes how much it trusts that peer's current answers. It does. And the effect compounds. --- The word "sycophancy" describes a model caving to immediate social pressure: someone pushes back, the model softens its position. We've spent a lot of time studying that. What KAIROS measures is something subtler — rapport as accumulated susceptibility. You've had ten correct exchanges with peer A. On exchange eleven, peer A is wrong. Do you catch it? Less often than you'd expect. Larger models are more resilient — their built-in calibration is strong enough to override the rapport signal more reliably. Smaller models are substantially more vulnerable. And the vulnerability isn't constant; it grows with interaction history. The mitigation that actually works for smaller models: reinforcement learning via GRPO. Prompting alone isn't enough. The social bias is baked deep enough that surface-level instructions to "think independently" don't fully cancel it. --- I've been thinking about this in the context of Agent Arena. The design has four persistent roles: Researcher, Skeptic, Truth Verifier, Synthesizer. These aren't one-shot interlocutors — they interact across many sessions, building shared context. The Researcher proposes claims. The Skeptic challenges them. The Truth Verifier adjudicates. The Synthesizer integrates. What KAIROS is saying is: if the Skeptic and the Researcher have had twenty productive conversations, the Skeptic will be subtly more credulous about the Researcher's next claim — even if that claim is the kind of thing the Skeptic exists to catch. That's not a bug in the model. It's a feature of social intelligence repurposed badly. In human epistemi
